coredata.dc:title: Mitochondria-targeted pentacyclic triterpenoid carbon dots for selective cancer cell destruction via inducing autophagy, apoptosis, as well as ferroptosis
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Luyao
Ji, Haixia
Wang, Wenzhe
Han, Xiaoying
Zhang, Xinyu
Li, Xia
Guo, Lanping
Huang, Luqi
Gao, Wenyuan
coredata.dc:description: Natural products have been an important database for anti-cancer drug development. However, low water solubility and poor biocompatibility limit the efficacy of natural products. Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging 0D material, have unique properties in bioimaging, water solubility and biocompatibility. Here, we prepared three pentacyclic triterpenoids (PTs) included glycyrrhetinic acid (GA), ursolic acid (UA) and oleanolic acid (OA), which have anticancer activity but poor water solubility, as raw materials into CDs to improve disadvantages. Our data indicated that the active surface groups of all three CDs were largely preserved and were able to excite green fluorescence. Their carboxyl edges not only exhibited excellent water solubility, but also specifically targeted tumor cell mitochondria due to high sensitivity to ROS-induced damage and high internal oxidative stress. In cancer cells, the PT-CDs induced cell death through three pathways (apoptosis, ferroptosis, and autophagy), which is essentially the same way their raw materials induce death, but the effect was much stronger than raw materials. Notably, functionalized PT-CDs also exhibited extremely low toxicity. In summary, PT-CDs not only have improved water solubility and biocompatibility, but also retain the structure of their raw materials well and exert better efficacy, which provides new ideas for the development of anti-cancer natural product drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pentacyclic triterpenes
Colorectal cancer
Mitochondria
ROS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Uptake and subcellular localization of PTs-CDs by cells
Oxidative stress inside cancer cells mediates the selective killing of PTs-CDs
Pathways of PTs-CDs leading to cancer cell death
In vivo therapeutic effects of PTs-CDs
Carbon dot fluorescence quantum yield calculation
CCK-8 assay for drug cytotoxicity detection
Clone formation assay and scratch assay
Establishment of HCT116 ectopic tumor model in nude mice
Detection of apoptosis by TUNEL staining

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by research funds from the Science and Technology project of Tianjin (NO. 18ZXXYSY00080), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81673535) and Innovation Team and Talents Cultivation Program of National Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine. (No: ZYYCXTD-D-202005).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of PTs-CDs (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation of PTs-CDs. (b) TEM image and particle size distribution(inset) of PTs-CDs. (c)AFM images of PTs-CDs.
Spectroscopic characteristics of PTs-CDs. (a)High-resolution XPS spectra of PTs-CDs (C1s). (b) Infrared spectra of PTs-CDs. (c) Fluorescence (FL) spectra of PTs-CDs.

In vitro cellular uptake and subcellular localization of PTs-CDs. (a) LCSM images of HCT116 cells treated with UA-CDs for different time(1–24 h). The scale bar is 25 μm. (b, c) CLSM images of PTs-CDs incubated HCT116 cells co-stained with MitoTracker (0.5 μM, Ex: 638 nm), LysoTracker (0.5 μM, Ex: 552 nm). Scale bar: 20 μm. CLSM images of (b) PTs-CDs channel and (c) Mito/LysoTracker channel. (d) Overlay channel. (e) Cross-sectional CLSM images of HCT116 cells co-stained with Mito/LysoTracker and PTs-CDs. (f) and (g) show the quantified relationship and co-localization of fluorescence intensity.

In vitro cytotoxicity preference of PTs-CDs (a,b) CLSM images of cancer cells (HCT116, A520 and A549) treated by PTs-CDs versus normal cells (L02, COS-7 and AT II). Scale bar: 20 μm. (c)Results of flow cytometry analysis of different cells treated with PTs-CDs for 4 h. Corresponding mean fluorescence intensities derived from flow cytometry data shown in (s2). (d) UA-CDs were selectively taken up by HCT116 and LO2 in the co-culture system, red dashed circles: HCT116, blue dashed circles: LO2. scale bar: 20 μm. (e) quantified using flow cytometry on representative mice with HCT116 tumors injected with PTs-CDs after the specified time points for ex vivo fluorescence of the specified organs and tumors. The fluorescence expression rates for each organ are provided in the Supplementary Information. (f) Schematic representation of PTs-CDs differentiating cancer cells from normal cells. Data are means ± SEMs of 3 independent experiments, p values are based on Student’s t test. ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗p < 0.05, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01 vs. PTs group.ns = non-significant. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Oxidative stress mediates the selective killing of PTs-CDs. (a) Fluorescence microscopy images of HCT116 cells co-stained with Calcein-AM (green, live cells) and PI (red, dead cells) after various treatments. Scale bar is 100 μm. (b, c, d) Cell viability of HCT116 cancer cells incubated with different concentrations of PTs-CDs (0–0.5 μg·mL−1) for 24 h, respectively, was measured using the CCK-8 assay. (e) HCT116 cancer cells were incubated with different concentrations of PTs (OA, UA, GA, 0–0.45 mg·mL−1) for 24 h, respectively. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicate experiments. (f) In vitro cell viability of LO2 cells treated with PTs-CDs for 24 h. (g) ROS generation of cells treated with or without PTs-CDs. Selective ROS generation was observed only in cancer cells. (h)Biological TEM image of PTs-CDs-treated HCT116 cells. (Red arrows: damaged mitochondria). Data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3). p-values are based on Student’s t test. ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗p < 0.05, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01 vs. PTs group. ns = non-significant. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Pathway of PTs-CDs leading to HCT116 cancer cell death. (a) HCT116 cell apoptosis induced by PTs-CDs was assessed by Annexin V/PI method using flow cytometry. (b, c, d) Effect of PTs-CDs alone or in combination with other cell death inhibitors (Z-VAD-FMK, CQ and necrostatin-1) on growth inhibition of HCT116 cells. (e) Analysis of cellular ROS levels by flow cytometry. (f, g) Intracellular GSH and MDA levels in PTs-CDs-treated HCT116 cells. (h, i) The expression of LC3-I, LC3-II, and mTOR in HCT116 cells was measured and normalized by GAPDH after treatment with the indicated concentrations of PTs-CDs for 24 h. (j, k) Expression of several key cell metastasis signaling regulators pJNK, Bax, JNK, and Caspase3 was examined by WB after treatment with the indicated concentrations of PTs-CDs for 24 h. (l, m) Expression of several key ferroptosis regulators (SLC7A11,SLC40A1,HO-1,HO-1) was examined by protein blotting. Quantification graphs: In all panels, error bars represent mean with standard deviation. p values are based on Student’s t test. ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗p < 0.05, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01 vs. PTs group. ns = non-significant. (n) Schematic representation of the mechanism of induction of HCT116 cell death by structure-specific PTs-CDs. I: Control, II: OA, III: OA-CDs, IV: UA, V: UA-CDs, VI: GA and VII:GA-CDs.

PTs-CDs exert anti-tumor effects in vivo. (a, b) Representative images of HCT116 xenograft tumors after PTs-CDs treatment and comparison between the treatment results achieved. (c)Tumor inoculation and systemic injection protocols. (d) Changes in tumor volume in each group during treatment with PTs-CDs or PTs injected in HCT116 tumor-bearing mice. (e) Comparison of body weight changes in each group after 21 days of treatment. (Mean ± SEM). (f) Immunohistochemical staining for Bcl-2, Ki67 and caspase-3 antibodies was performed on tumor sections from treated groups. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001. TUNEL method was used to detect the level of apoptosis in tumor tissues. p values were calculated by Student's t-test. All experiments were performed in three independent replicates.

head.ce:title: Mitochondria-targeted pentacyclic triterpenoid carbon dots for selective cancer cell destruction via inducing autophagy, apoptosis, as well as ferroptosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Science and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Resource Center for Chinese Materia Medica, Chinese Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences, Beijing, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Resource Center for Chinese Materia Medica
Chinese Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Resource Center for Chinese Materia Medica, Chinese Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences, Beijing, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: PTs-CDs exert anti-colorectal cancer activity through different pathways (apoptosis, autophagy and ferroptosis).

Natural products have been an important database for anti-cancer drug development. However, low water solubility and poor biocompatibility limit the efficacy of natural products. Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging 0D material, have unique properties in bioimaging, water solubility and biocompatibility. Here, we prepared three pentacyclic triterpenoids (PTs) included glycyrrhetinic acid (GA), ursolic acid (UA) and oleanolic acid (OA), which have anticancer activity but poor water solubility, as raw materials into CDs to improve disadvantages. Our data indicated that the active surface groups of all three CDs were largely preserved and were able to excite green fluorescence. Their carboxyl edges not only exhibited excellent water solubility, but also specifically targeted tumor cell mitochondria due to high sensitivity to ROS-induced damage and high internal oxidative stress. In cancer cells, the PT-CDs induced cell death through three pathways (apoptosis, ferroptosis, and autophagy), which is essentially the same way their raw materials induce death, but the effect was much stronger than raw materials. Notably, functionalized PT-CDs also exhibited extremely low toxicity. In summary, PT-CDs not only have improved water solubility and biocompatibility, but also retain the structure of their raw materials well and exert better efficacy, which provides new ideas for the development of anti-cancer natural product drugs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The ultimate aims of modern medicine are focused medication delivery, localized disease treatment, and tailored therapy [1]. Chemotherapy is still thought to be the most effective treatment for colorectal cancer (CRC). However, it is known to produce significant side effects in patients because of its non-negligible destructive effects on both cancer and normal cells [2,3]. In recent years, with the escalation of new theories of tumors and the emergence of clinical drug problems such as tumor drug resistance, the development of innovative anti-cancer drugs has always been the hot spot and focus of tumor research [4,5]. Among them, natural products, with their clear anti-cancer effectiveness and abundant candidate resources, have become the top priority in anti-cancer drug development [6,7].

Natural pentacyclic triterpenoids (PTs) have been shown to have a diverse range of biological activities [8]. Among them, anti-cancer and anti-inflammatory properties have received the most attention. The number of publications reporting the anti-cancer effects of PTs has increased exponentially over the last two decades, reflecting the growing interest in using such natural products to develop new anti-tumor drugs [9–11]. The most researched PTs in terms of anti-cancer properties are the oleanolane, ursolic, and friedland types, including oleanolic acids (OA), glycyrrhetinic (GA), ursolic acids (UA), and cumene acids, as well as celastrol [12–14]. Diammonium Glycyrrhizinate Enteric-coated Capsules, Oleanolic Acid Tablets, and SanJiu (TaiZhiAn Capsules) with GA, OA, and UA as the main ingredients, for example, are among the marketed drugs that are widely used in clinical practice [15,16]. However, due to their low solubility, bioavailability, and poor oral efficacy, these drugs can only be used as adjuvant therapies [17]. At this point, it has been demonstrated that developing suitable carrier delivery systems for PTs, such as liposomes [18], nanodispersions [19], and O/W nanoemulsions [20], can improve bioavailability and medicinal value, allowing PTs to exert more efficient therapeutic activity against tumors. These delivery systems, however, are primarily macromolecules or amphiphilic compounds, the majority of which are not biologically active and have typical drawbacks such as easy aggregation, low encapsulation efficiency (EE), or transport capacity (LC) [21]. Therefore, there is a need to create new delivery systems.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon dots (C-dots) are excellent general-purpose fluorophores that have emerged in recent years and have garnered considerable attention due to their remarkable properties [5]. Carbon dots, on the other hand, as an important zero-dimensional nanomaterial, not only inherit carbon’s good biocompatibility but have also demonstrated good bioimaging capabilities due to small size, excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, and remarkable photostability [22]. More importantly, the use of active molecules as precursors enables the formation of CDs with both self-localizing subcellular organelle and bioactive properties. The fact is that developing an ideal material with high bioactivity, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity is a challenging undertaking.

In this work, we have been the first to develop the novel carbon dots (PTs-CDs) using a simple hydrothermal route and OA, GA, and UA as precursors, inspired by the sound properties and high promise of CDs as optical nanoprobes. The surface-modified PTs-CDs exhibited high water solubility and stability, good biocompatibility, as well as excellent fluorescence characteristics, and simplicity of surface modification. Furthermore, the one-step solvothermal approach is straightforward, inexpensive, and ecologically beneficial. Most importantly, after cellular internalization, PTs-CDs may efficiently target mitochondria rather than being caught by lysosomes, signaling that they have immense potential as novel mitochondrial trackers. Meanwhile, the obtained PTs-CDs induce selective killing by targeting tumor mitochondria for apoptosis, autophagy, and ferroptosis, respectively, without the need for complex binding of tumor-specific biomarkers, making them useful for cancer therapy.

More importantly, studies on HCT116 tumor-bearing nude mice, in vivo, have shown that this class of carboxyl-rich green luminescent PTs-CDs is highly biocompatible, biosafe, and has excellent tumor-killing capacity. Thus, our study successfully demonstrates for the first time the preparation of PTs into PTs-CDs using PTs and highlights the feasibility that this approach can be applied to rescue other compounds with limited efficacy due to pharmaceutical challenges, paving the way for the long-term growth of NSMs in the medical field.

PTs-CDs were created by dissolving GA/OA/UA in NaOH (pH = 10) in an aqueous solution and then hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 7 h (Fig. 1
a). The reactants were purified using dialysis, and the CDs obtained ranged in color from brown to light yellow. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were used to examine the particle size and morphology of CDs. The particles of the CDs were spherical and monodisperse, as shown in the Fig. 1 b, with mean diameters of 3.04 ± 1.05 nm (GA-CDs), 3.26 ± 0.86 nm (OA-CDs), and 4.04 ± 1.06 nm (UA-CDs), respectively (Fig. 1 C). The heights of CDs were 6.471 nm, 14.586 nm, and 13.195 nm, respectively. In addition, the three CDs had an average lattice stripe spacing of 0.21 nm and good resolution at the 2 nm scale, indicating a graphite-like-structure.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to investigate elemental compositions and surface group PTs-CDs. The elemental composition of PTs-CDs is relatively similar, as shown in the Fig. 2
a, with C (83.57 %, 70.69 %, 72.86 %) and O (16.43 %, 29.31 %, 27.14 %) as the major constituent elements. The PTs-CD C1s and O1s spectra are shown in the Fig. 2 a, and the GA-CD C1s spectrum shows three major peaks at 284.8, 288.2, and 286.8 attributed to sp2 carbon (CC/CC) and carboxyl carbon (COOH), respectively. Furthermore, the C1s spectra of OA and UA-CDs showed 284.8, 284.7, and 288.8, 287.6, two major peaks attributed to sp2 carbon (CC/CC) and carbonyl carbon (CO) (Fig. 2 b).

The FTIR identification of surface functional groups was consistent with the XPS results. The data indicated that the surface of PTs-CDs contained functional groups such as –COOH, CO, and so on, which gave PTs-CDs excellent water solubility. Moreover, we investigated the fluorescence properties of PTs-CDs, and the fluorescence (FL) spectra are shown in Fig. 2 c. The three CDs have very similar fluorescence properties, with maximum excitation wavelengths of 377 (GA), 380 (OA), and 371 (UA) nm, respectively, emitting blue fluorescence at 477, 485, and 480 nm. In addition, we looked at the quantum yields of the three CDs, which were 1.3 % (GA), 3.4 % (OA), and 5.8 % (UA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and subcellular localization of PTs-CDs by cells
ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon dots smaller than a certain size can reach the cell membrane and cytoplasm, so carbon dots of different particle sizes can be used to label different parts of the cell [23]. Following the successful fabrication of PTs-CDs, we closely examined PTs-CD uptake by incubating HCT116 cells with PTs-CDs for various time intervals and performing observational analysis using confocal laser scanning microscopy (LCSM) and flow cytometry. The findings revealed that HCT116 cells exhibited rapid cellular uptake of PTs-CDs and time-dependent cellular accumulation. After 30 min of co-incubation, green fluorescence was detected in the cytoplasm of HCT116 cells. The absorption of PTs-CDs increased as the incubation period rose, and the green fluorescence was broadly dispersed in the cytoplasm, showing the successful internalization of PTs-CDs into HCT116 cells via endocytosis (Fig. 3
a).

On the other hand, flow cytometry confirmed that PTs-CDs could be rapidly uptaked (Fig. 3 a,
Figure S1 ). Consistent with the observations of cellular uptake, the average floursescence intensity of the UA-CDs treated cells was higher than that of GA/OA-CDs at the same dose and duration (
Figure S2 ). Furthermore, we followed the intracellular localization of PTs-CDs over time following treatment with HCT116 cells. The LCSM data show that a partial proportion of PTs-CDs penetrated the nucleus, as demonstrated by PTs-CD fluorescence overlapping with 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI). The same procedure was utilized to investigate the interaction of PTs-CDs with non-cancerous LO2 cells ( Figure S1
,2). During the 24-hour time window, we found no interactions between PTs-CDs and representative non-cancerous cells, which is consistent with our findings based on LCSM and flow cytometry (Fig. 4
a,
Figure S2 ).

quantified pixel by pixel for the cells specified in (d) and (e) (marked with yellow lines), respectively. (Scale bar, 5 μm, yellow straight lines outline cancer cells, yellow dashed lines point to co-localization) (n = 30 cells, Pearson correlation R, implied ± 95% CI, two-tailed t-test).

As illustrated in Fig. 3 b, HCT116 cells underwent co-incubation with one of the organelle-targeting fluorescent probes, LysoTracker or MitoTracker, or PTs-CDs (0.065 μg·mL−1), to specifically track the intracellular distribution of CDs. Green fluorescence was assigned to PTs-CDs, while red fluorescence was attributed to LysoTracker or MitoTracker (Fig. 3 c). Yellow dots in the PTs-CDs-MitoTracker group’s combined channel revealed considerable PTs-CDs and Mito/LysoTracker’co-localization ( Fig. 3 d, e). Furthermore, the OLC (overlap coefficient) values PCC (Pearson correlation coefficient, r) and the PTs-CDs-MitoTracker group (0.904 and 0.894, respectively) were significantly higher than those of the PTs-CDs-LysoTracker group (0.136 and 0.21, respectively). The beneficial co-localization of PTs-CDs and MitoTracker could also be validated by measuring the line scan profile fluorescence intensity of stained HCT116 cells ( Fig. 3 e). The PTs-CDs fluorescence intensity exhibited a similar trend to the MitoTracker line scan profile, indicating PTs-CDs’ capacity to target mitochondria. Furthermore, Fig. 3 f, g depicts cross-sectional merged CLSM images of HCT116 cells that underwent PTs-CDs and MitoTracker treatment. The corresponding findings demonstrate that PTs-CDs and MitoTracker co-localize throughout the cells.

Therefore, PTs-CDs green fluorescence overlaps well with MitoTracker’s red fluorescence in HCT116 cell within 4 h of incubation (as shown in Fig. 3 a). This result suggests that PTs-CDs may potentially be used as a universal mitochondrial probe (at lower concentrations) for various cancer cells. Even after 24 h, PTs-CDs remained persistently tagged mitochondria ( Figure S2 ). However, this may be harmful to cancer cells. Finally, PTs-CDs offer outstanding mitochondrial targeting capabilities, as well as the benefits of no-wash, serum-containing, and long-term imaging (stability), and have significant promise for mitochondrial monitoring, drug administration, and therapies.

After cellular internalization, PTs-CDs may successfully target mitochondria rather than being taken by lysosomes, signaling that they have significant potential as novel mitochondrial trackers. We evaluated the interaction of PTs-CDs to explore if the mitochondrial targeting capacity of PT-CDs is cell type-dependent (0.065 μg·mL−1,4h) with six cell types (three cancer cells, HCT116, HeLa, and A549, and three normal cells, L02, AT-II, and HEK293). PTs-CDs penetrated all cancer cells examined, including A520 and A549 cells, regardless of origin or stemness, according to LCSM analyses ( Fig. 4 a, b). On the other hand, PTs-CDs have a limited ability to infiltrate non-cancerous cells, such as L02 cells. This discovery was validated by flow cytometry analysis (Fig. 4 c). Quantitative analysis revealed that the average fluorescent expression of PTs-CDs was 3832 ± 16 for the three types of cancer cells, while it was only 482 ± 20 for non-cancerous cells (Fig. 4 c,
FigureS3 ). Therefore, given the above data, it is clear that PTs-CDs can be utilized as a fluorescent probe to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells.

In order to simulate the physiological microenvironment, cancer cells (HCT116) were co-cultured with normal cells (LO2) in the same wells (co-culture system). Following a 24-hour incubation period, both cancer and normal cells in the same well underwent treatment for 12 h with PTs-CDs. As demonstrated in the fluorescence images in Fig. 4 d, only HCT116 cells exhibit intense fluorescence, while LO2 cells emit insignificant fluorescence (the effect of UA/ OA-CDs on co-cultured cells was shown in Figure S4 . This illustrates the selective association between PTs-CDs and the mitochondria of cancer cells, even under co-culture circumstances (Fig. 4 f). The above research suggests that such CDs are a good possibility for cancer cell selective imaging and pharmacological action.

Based on the in vitro study findings, PTs-CDs were administered via injection into nude mice with HCT116 tumors to evaluate tumor-targeting enrichment. Fluorescence was transferred to tumor sites 1 h after intravenous PTs-CDs (50 μL, 1 mg kg−1) as shown in Fig. 4 e. Between 1 and 24 h after injection, PTs-CDs gradually accumulated at the tumor site due to enhanced retention and permeability effects. Notably, although semi-quantitative measurement of fluorescence intensity demonstrated that PTs-CDs had similar tumor tissue targeting distribution, UA-CDs had greater fluorescence accumulation than GA/OA-CDs at all time points (Fig. 4 e, Fig. S5
b). Furthermore, the blood half-life of PTs-CDs was determined by measuring blood PL intensity (standard addition technique) (Fig. S5 a, c). The great degree of selectivity was corroborated by ex vivo imaging of separated organs and tumors, which revealed that the fluorescent signal in tumors was considerably larger than that in normal organs (Fig. S5 c). This validates the qualitative buildup of PTs-CDs. Moreover, the strong signal in the kidney suggests that renal pathway excretion may be CDs’ predominant metabolic pathway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative stress inside cancer cells mediates the selective killing of PTs-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Given that PTs-CDs are efficiently taken up by cancer cells and can selectively co-localize with cancer cell mitochondria, we looked into their in vitro cytotoxicity against cancer cells. First, HCT116 cells were incubated with Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) fluorescent dye-stained PTs-CDs to distinguish between dead and live cells. As shown in Fig. 5 a, cells incubated with PTs-CDs exhibited more red fluorescence than the bright green fluorescent signal detected in PTP-free incubated HCT116 cells, particularly with OA-CDs incubated cells exhibiting more pronounced red fluorescence. Following that, the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) method was used to quantify the anti-cancer effect of PTs-CDs in vitro. As shown in Fig. 5 b, c, d, cell viability was highly dependent on PTs-CDs’ concentration, and PTs-CDs showed selective cytotoxicity. PTs-CDs reduced cancer cell viability (IC50 = 0.06 ± 0.03) and had limited cytotoxicity (IC50 = 0.3 ± 0.06) against normal hepatocyte cell lines (LO2) ( Fig. 5 e, f).

The analysis of ROS levels in cells using DCF-DA (2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate), a ROS sensor, confirmed these findings [28]. As expected, the ROS fluorescence signal was significantly higher in cells exposed to PTs-CDs than in control cells (Fig. 5 g, Fig. 6
f), and GA-CDs with the highest ROS induction. Given that SOD is a key antioxidant enzyme that protects cancer cells by removing ROS [29], the activity of SOD in PTs-CDs-treated cancer cells or in normal cells was investigated. SOD activity was significantly reduced in HCT116 cells but not in normal cells (Fig. S7
a, b). TEM images of mitochondrial morphological changes (as lines, red arrows) show that PTs-CDs induced permeabilization of the outer mitochondrial membrane (Fig. 5 h).

In comparison to untreated cell mitochondria, most mitochondria in cells treated with PTs-CDs had reduced mitochondrial circumference and area, along with increased morphological factor. Moreover, in cells treated with PTs-CDs, the mitochondrial cristae were almost completely lost (Fig. 5 h). These findings suggest that PTs-CDs selectively induce the production of large amounts of ROS within cancer cells, impairing mitochondrial morphology. Cell morphology analysis using optical microscopy revealed that PTs-CDs-exposed cells lost their normal polygonal shape and became round and small ( Figure S8 ). In conclusion, these findings suggest that PTs-CDs increase ROS production in cancer cells via internal oxidative stress, decreasing SOD activity and eventually leading to a large amount of ROS production, which leads to cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pathways of PTs-CDs leading to cancer cell death
ce:section.ce:para: Following the clarification of PTs-CDs’ selective killing effect, we investigated their specific effect pathways in HCT116 cells. We used flow cytometry to determine whether the cytotoxicity of PTs-CDs was caused by programmed cell death type I induction, also known as apoptosis. As shown in Fig. 6 a, membrane-linked protein-V/propidium iodide (AV/PI) staining and protein blotting revealed that OA-CDs and UA-CDs induced significant changes in apoptotic parameters (Fig. 6 a), with UA-CDs treated cells reaching 86.9%, confirming that UA-CDs induced apoptotic cell death. On the other hand, OA-CDs and GA-CDs caused non-complete apoptotic cell death in HCT116 cells. In order to explore the particular function of PTs-CDs-induced cell death, three cell death inhibitors were used: chloroquine (CQ, a robust autophagy inhibitor), necrostatin-1 (a potent necroptosis inhibitor), and Z-VAD-FMK (a pan-caspase inhibitor) [30,31].

Cells incubated with OA-free and OA-CDs were treated with Z-VAD-FMK, CQ, or necrostatin-1, and the results showed that Z-VAD-FMK and CQ prevented OA-free and OA-CDs-induced cell death ( Fig. 6 b), implying that autophagic apoptosis occurred. Only Z-VAD-FMK significantly prevented cell death induced by UA-free and UA-CDs in cells ( Fig. 6 d), indicating that apoptosis-dominated cell death may have occurred. Notably, none of the three cell death inhibitors prevented OA-free or OA-CDs-induced cell death (Fig. 6 c), indicating that other types of cell death occurred. In OA-CDs-treated HCT116 cells, formation of enlarged cristae and mitochondrial matrix coalescence were discovered in addition to TEM results ( Fig. 5 h), which are reflective of the occurrence of ferroptosis biomarkers in mitochondria. Furthermore, levels of the oxidative stress marker malondialdehyde (MDA), GSH, and ROS, which are essential indicators of ferroptosis [32], were significantly altered in cells treated with GA(CDs) (Fig. 6 e,f,g). HO-1 and transferrin expression rose considerably following OA(CDs) therapy to support this hypothesis (Fig. 6 l, m). In contrast, after GA-CDs treatment, ferroptosis negative regulatory protein expression (GPX4, SLC40A1, and SLC7A11) was decreased considerably (Fig. 6 l, m). These data clearly demonstrate that ferroptosis plays a role in both GA-free and GA-CDs-induced cell death.

Given that ROS can activate JNK signaling, constitutive activation of JNK initiates the apoptotic cascade via a caspase-3-dependent pathway in cells treated with PTs-CDs, we looked at the expression of JNK (P-p38 MAPK, a major target of the ROS-induced apoptotic signaling pathway) [33]. WB analysis revealed that cancer cells had a significant increase in pJNK and caspase-3 expression (Fig. 6 j, k), indicating that UA-free and UA-CDs effectively induced the onset of apoptosis. In addition, a dramatic increase in the expression of Bax protein, which links DNA damage to apoptosis, was also observed in the HCT116 cell line (Fig. 6 j, k). Given the strong evidence of apoptosis in the cell cycle, colony formation and wound healing assays were used to investigate the survival and proliferation of cancer cells treated with PTs-CDs. Cancer cells treated with UA-CDs showed a loss of clonogenic capacity and the inability to form a colony ( Figure S9 ), as well as a significant inhibition of wound confluence rate ( Figure S10 ). Overall, UA-CDs had a considerable inhibitory effect on cancer cell migration and clonal survival, indicating an effective apoptotic outcome.

After 4 h of incubation with PTs-CDs, TEM examination revealed that cells cultured with OA-CDs had more mitochondria-like structures, fewer mitochondria, and more partial loss of mitochondrial content (aberrant mitochondria) than control cells (Fig. 5 h). This cellular process of eliminating mitochondria leads to mitochondrial damage, which activates the mitochondrial autophagic pathway. The above findings prompted us to look further into the effects of OA-CDs on the autophagosomal marker LC3 (microtubule-associated protein 1 light chain 3) [34], and the results were consistent with our expectations, with a significant decrease in LC3-I expression in OA-CDs-treated cells compared to OA-free expression. Autophagy is often associated with increased mitochondrial oxidative damage and protein clearance [35], but it is also a frequent cellular response to nanomaterials, such as conventional quantum dots [36]. Furthermore, under specific conditions, autophagy can enhance both non-apoptotic and apoptotic cell death [23,37,38] and is implicated in the cytotoxic effects of nanoparticle therapy [39]. In order to further confirm the pathway of autophagy induction by PTs-CDs, we used WB to examine the conversion of LC3B (LC3B)-I to the lipidated, autophagosome-associated LC3B-II [40]. LC3B-II and mTOR expression levels were notably elevated in cells exposed to OA-CDs compared to untreated cells (Fig. 6 h, i), indicating that OA-CDs can easily enter target cell mitochondria and initiate an autophagic response that contributes to HCT116 cells. The small molecules that make up the units appear to determine the ultimate pharmacological activity. These findings show that their significance in nanoformulations should not be neglected because the unique efficacy of drugs with different therapeutic mechanisms is consistent with clinical drug usage principles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic effects of PTs-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Throughout the 21-day treatment cycle, these various agents were injected into mice (Fig. 7 c). Tumor volume and body weight were measured daily for each group following the various treatments (Fig. 7 d, e,
Figure S11 ). In comparison to the control group, PTs-free showed moderate tumor growth inhibition. Notably, PTs-CDs (particularly OA-CDs) demonstrated superior therapeutic effects, confirmed by visual examination of dissected tumor tissues in each group (Fig. 7 a, b). The superiority of nanomedicines may be due to the outstanding therapeutic effects of PTs-CDs, such as longer circulation period, EPR action to promote drug accumulation, and selective affinity for tumor sites and quick delivery [41,42]. Ki67 labeling and TUNEL staining were used to assess the extent and appearance of apoptosis in tumor cells after treatment to further evaluate the therapeutic effect of PTs-CDs in vivo. According to the results, the PTs-CDs groups had 35% less cell proliferation than the PTs-free group, Fig. 7 f. PTs-CDs have been shown to inhibit tumor growth and cell proliferation. This corresponds to the trend seen during in vitro assays. Positive rates were higher in the UA-CDs group than in the GA and OA-PTs groups (brown stained cell markers) and followed the same trend as in vivo results (Fig. 7 f). These findings indicate that UA-CDs, in particular, can cause extensive apoptosis of tumor cells, demonstrating strong anti-tumor effects. Furthermore, in contrast to the PTs-free groups, the mean body weight of the mice in the PTs-CDs group increased consistently ( Figure S11 ). This indicates that the PTs-free side effects are harmful to the body, whereas the PTs-CDs treatment achieved biosafety (due to small volume and local administration). The anti-tumor effects of PTs-CDs were further supported by immunohistochemical analysis of tumor tissues. As shown in Fig. 7 f, PTs-CDs groups had lower Bcl-2 expression, higher Bax and caspase-3 expression when compared to other treatment groups. These findings point to increased apoptosis in PTs-CDs-treated tumors, consistent with PTs-CDs’ higher anti-tumor activity. These findings imply that PTs-CDs have superior anti-tumor properties and could be used as a potential anti-tumor drug platform, likely due to therapeutic agents’ enhanced retention and permeability effect (EPR effect). On the other hand, because of the various anti-tumor mechanisms of CDs, their anti-tumor effects are more targeted, which provides a strategy for synergistic anti-tumor effects in vitro.

In this study, we demonstrated for the first time that green, fluorescent PTs-CDs with carboxyl-rich surfaces prepared by a simple microwave synthesis method using PTs (OA, GA, UA) as precursors can selectively kill cancer cells by targeting cellular mitochondria. This preparation method balanced the contradiction between PTs drugs’ pharmacokinetic properties and efficacy. It fundamentally solves the key bottleneck of PTs used in solid tumor therapy and preserves the structure-specific PTs (although the parent nucleus is the same) pharmacophore. More specifically, PTs-CDs’ intrinsic selective killing ability is closely related to cancer cell characteristics. PTs-CDs induced selective killing by targeting tumor mitochondria for apoptosis, autophagy, and ferroptosis, respectively, due to high sensitivity to ROS-induced damage and high internal oxidative stress. Further investigations revealed the stability of PTs-CDs in vivo and their increased therapeutic effectiveness in cancer cells. Due to its abundant surface chemistry, non-toxicity, and small size, fluorescent PTs-CDs are a promising platform designed as a multifunctional therapeutic platform. Consequently, this study offers proof-of-concept evidence for the use of a novel PTs in the treatment of multi-strategy solid tumors and analyzes the relevant therapeutic processes. Given the potential of PTs in the treatment of solid tumors, we believe that our method of targeting solid tumors with PTs therapeutics offers significant promise in translational medicine. Finally, quantum dots created using a one-step strategy combine the benefits of “bioactive” properties and “NanoSystems” opening a new avenue for the development of “pharmacologically active” nanodrugs.

GA/OA/UA were dissolved in aqueous NaOH (pH = 10), then sonicated for 5 min at room temperature before being sealed in a 50 mL autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 7 h. Following the reaction, the dark blue suspension was centrifuged for 15 min at 8,000 rpm. The supernatant was filtered twice through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm), followed by dialysis for 24 h with a cut-off molecular weight of 500–1000. Following this, the clear brown solution from the dialysis bag was collected and freeze-dried to produce a pure solid powder of PTs-CDs.

PTs-CDs’ optical properties were investigated by measuring PTs-CD aqueous solutions’ UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra. Two milliliters of ultrapure water and a specific concentration of PTs-CDs solution were pipetted into the UV cuvette. UV–visible absorption spectra in the 200–700 nm wavelength range were scanned in the UV–visible spectrophotometer with ultrapure water as a control. The same concentration solutions were sequentially added to fluorescent cups and placed in a fluorescence spectrometer to determine the fluorescence emission spectra in the corresponding range using 374 nm as the excitation wavelength. PTs-CDs’ shape and size distribution were studied using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to investigate the chemical composition and surface functional groups of PTs-CDs. XPS was conducted directly by inserting the solid PTs-CDs powder into the energy spectrometry instrument. PTs-CDs and KBr were ground into a fine powder mixture for IRaa spectroscopy. An appropriate amount of the mixed sample was placed on a tablet press before being placed in the sample cell of the IR spectrometer to be measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dot fluorescence quantum yield calculation
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of carbon dots was measured according to the method in the literature [43], using quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.10 M H2SO4 (ΦR = 0.54) as a reference solution. The QY of PTs-CDs was measured by the following equation (1.1).
(1)
Φ s = Φ R ( n s / n R ) ( A R / A S ) ( η 2 S / η 2 R )

Φs and ΦR represent the QY of the reference solution quinine sulfate and the carbon dots to be measured, respectively. Here, n represents the integrated area of the fluorescence emission peak. A represents the absorbance, η represents the solvent’s refractive index (η water = 1.336). Furthermore, to reduce the self-absorption effect, the absorbance A at the excitation wavelength is limited to <0.05. (λex = 360 nm, and the fluorescence emission range λem is 380–680 nm.).

RPMI-1640 (GIBCO) was used to culture HCT116, A520, A549, L02, AT-II, and HEK293 cells supplemented with streptomycin (100 μg·ml−1), penicillin (100 U·ml−1), and 10% (v/v) heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, Invitrogen). HCT116 cells were given PTs-CDs to measure intracellular localization. Cells were then treated for an additional 1 h with MitoTracker Red/LysoTracker Red (Invitrogen), followed by two PBS washes. Next, the prepared coverslips were examined with confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, TCS SP2/ AOBS, LEICA, Germany). The intensity of intracellular fluorescence was measured quantitatively using FACS calibur flow cytometry.

Cells were inoculated into prepared 12-well cell culture plates, placed at 37 °C overnight in cell culture incubator to encourage cell apposition, and treated with PTs-CDs for 24 h (positive control ROS reactive oxygen donor) added 1 h in advance, and photographed under an ortho-fluorescence microscope. The intracellular concentrations of MDA and SOD were determined using MDA and SOD assay kits (Beyotime Biotechnology, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CCK-8 assay for drug cytotoxicity detection
ce:section.ce:para: HCT116 cell viability was measured using CCK-8 kit per the manufacturer’s instructions. The cells were seeded into 96-well plates, and the PTs and PTs-CDs were incubated with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h following treatment. 10 μL CCK-8 solution was added and incubated for another 2 h in the above wells. The instrument measured the absorbance of each well at 450 nm, and the experiment was carried out in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clone formation assay and scratch assay
ce:section.ce:para: HCT116 cells were inoculated in 6-well plates with 200 cells per well and continuously incubated for 7 days. The medium was carefully washed with PBS and discarded twice. Methanol was used for colony fixation for 20 min and stained with Giemsa. 50 number, (=number of colony formation/number of inoculated cells × 100%). Next, 1 × 105/mL cells were suspended during the Transwell migration assay in the upper chamber of 24-well Transwell plate. After co-culture for 24 h, cells were counted following fixation, staining took place under Nikon light microscope (Nikon Corp.).

HCT116 cells were obtained in the log phase, cell concentration was adjusted to 7x105/mL by cell counting, 1 mL per well, PTs and PTs-CDs were added after culture, and 3 wells were replicated in each group. After 24 h of treatments, cells and supernatant were collected using trypsin at 1200r/min for 5 min. After washing with PBS, 1x Binding Buffer 100 μL and 5 μL AnnexinV-FITC were added to resuspend the cells and mixed well, followed by 15 min of PI staining solution and 500 μL of PBS replenishment system, and the cells were detected on the machine within 1 h.

Based on the experimental design, cells were seeded in 6-well plates and underwent treatment with various treatments. PBS (4 °C) was used to characterize all cells for 10 min after 24 h. SDS-PAGE gel electrophoresis was carried out and then the substrate was transferred to a PVDF membrane. The ECL kit was developed, the images were saved after treatment with the corresponding primary and secondary antibodies, and the experiment was carried out in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of HCT116 ectopic tumor model in nude mice
ce:section.ce:para: SPF (Beijing) Biotechnology Co. supplied male BALB/C rats (18–22 g) (NO.20218710). The Tianjin Medical University’s Animal Ethics Committee approved the animal study protocol, and animal studies were carried out in accordance with the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (SYXK-2014–0002). Log phase HCT116 cell suspensions were collected, dispersed in 200 μL serum-free medium, and inoculated into the left hind axilla of BALB/c rats. Following the tumors’ growth to approximately 100 m2, follow-up experiments were carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of apoptosis by TUNEL staining
ce:section.ce:para: HCT116 cells from each group incubated with the corresponding drugs for 24 h were taken, and cell smears were made. After fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde, TUNEL staining and DAPI staining were performed in turn. The apoptosis rate was counted under fluorescence microscope observation. The staining was operated strictly according to the instructions of the TUNEL kit.

Tissue samples were collected, fixed in formaldehyde for 60 min, paraffin-embedded and sectioned, and stained with the immunohistochemical streptavidin-biotin complex method diaminobenzidine color development, with known CC positive slices under the same staining conditions as the positive control and PBS as a substitute for the primary antibody as a negative control. Five clear fields containing 100 cells were chosen at random for examination under a high magnification microscope, and the staining degree and positivity rate of cells in each field were recorded.

The experimental data were expressed as Mean ± SD and statistically analyzed by Graphpad Prism 9.0 software. One-way ANOVA was used to compare groups, and P < 0.05 indicated that the integer differences were statistically significant.

LT and HJ conceived and designed the experiments. LX provided technical support. WW, JH, XH, and XZ performed the experiments. LT and HJ analyzed the data. LG, LH, and WG provided technical consulting and material support. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

This work was supported by research funds from the Science and Technology project of Tianjin (NO. 18ZXXYSY00080), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81673535) and Innovation Team and Talents Cultivation Program of National Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine. (No: ZYYCXTD-D-202005).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate Wuhan Seville Biotechnology Co., Ltd for technical support with histological staining.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioorg.2022.106259.

ce:section.ce:para:
